explanation:
The natural language specification says: 'If the value of signal RWs_angular_momentum is greater than 0.35, then the value of signal RWs_torque shall be equal to 0.' Given the available atomic propositions, this can be interpreted as: whenever 'RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35' holds, then 'RWs_torque_equalTo_0' must also hold at the same time. In LTL, this is typically captured as 'Globally, if p then q', i.e., G(p -> q). This guarantees that at all times, whenever 'RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35' is true, 'RWs_torque_equalTo_0' is also true. 

Any alternative encoding, such as using F or U, would not capture the requirement that this implication must hold at every time step for the duration of the execution.

The natural language requirement states that whenever the RWs_angular_momentum is greater than 0.35, then RWs_torque must be equal to 0. In LTL, this can be captured with the implication: globally, if 'RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35' holds, then 'RWs_torque_equalTo_0' must also hold at that time. This is expressed as G (RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 -> RWs_torque_equalTo_0). This formula ensures that at all points in the future, whenever the angular momentum condition is true, the torque condition is also true, accurately reflecting the input requirement, and it does this without using any numeric comparisons, only using the provided atomic propositions.

The input natural language states that if the value of RWs_angular_momentum is greater than 0.35, then the value of RWs_torque shall be equal to 0. In LTL, an implication that whenever one condition is true, another must also be true, is expressed as G (p -> q), where p is the antecedent and q is the consequent. Here, whenever RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 holds, it must be the case that RWs_torque_equalTo_0 holds at the same time. The globally operator G ensures this check holds for every moment in time. Numerical comparison operators are not allowed, so atomic propositions that encode this information (e.g., RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35) are used instead. This translation correctly captures the natural language intent without numerical operators. Other forms such as using 'until' or temporal sequencing are not justified here since the requirement is not about a future response, but an invariant condition.

The requirement states that whenever 'RWs angular momentum is greater than 0.35' holds, then 'RWs torque is equal to 0' must hold as well. In LTL, this type of safety constraint is most naturally captured using the globally operator combined with implication: G(a -> b), where a is the condition and b is the required consequence. Thus, 'G(RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 -> RWs_torque_equalTo_0)' expresses that at all times, if 'RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35' is true, then 'RWs_torque_equalTo_0' must also be true at that moment. No previously given LTL properties are present for comparison.

The natural language states that if 'RWs_angular_momentum' is greater than 0.35, then 'RWs_torque' must be equal to 0. Since only the provided atomic propositions can be used, the LTL must use 'RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35' and 'RWs_torque_equalTo_0'. The implication must hold globally: whenever 'RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35' is true, 'RWs_torque_equalTo_0' must also be true at the same time. This is expressed as G( RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 -> RWs_torque_equalTo_0 ). Previous LTLs that require historical or future guarantees might not capture the immediate implication over all time, which is what this LTL formula ensures.

The input natural language specifies a condition: whenever 'RWs angular momentum is greater than 0.35', then 'RWs torque shall be equal to 0'. This is a standard implication safety property and is directly mapped to 'Globally, if RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 then RWs_torque_equalTo_0'. No use of numerical comparison operators is needed, as the atomic propositions already encode the needed state. The property is thus: G (RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 -> RWs_torque_equalTo_0).

The property specifies that whenever the RWs_angular_momentum is greater than 0.35, the RWs_torque must be equal to 0 at the same moment and for all subsequent moments as long as the condition holds. This is captured in LTL as 'G (RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 -> RWs_torque_equalTo_0)', meaning globally, if RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 is true, then RWs_torque_equalTo_0 must also be true. This best captures the implication in the natural language. Previous LTL encodings might have tried to use 'U' or 'F' operators to relate the timing, but unless a change in state is expected, this direct implication is correct. No previous LTLs are referenced for deficiencies in this case.

The input states that whenever RWs angular momentum is greater than 0.35, RWs torque must be equal to 0. This can be represented in LTL as 'G (RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 -> RWs_torque_equalTo_0)', meaning that globally, if at any time the first condition is true, the second must also hold at the same time. This captures the natural language requirement as an invariant implication.

The natural language says: 'If the value of signal RWs_angular_momentum is greater than 0.35, then the value of signal RWs_torque shall be equal to 0.' We are provided atomic propositions that encode 'RWs angular momentum is greater than 0.35' and 'RWs torque is equal to 0.' The correct LTL property should capture that whenever the proposition indicating that angular momentum is above threshold holds, it is required that the proposition indicating zero torque holds at that time as well. This is best modeled as a safety property: globally, whenever RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 holds, RWs_torque_equalTo_0 must also hold. In LTL, this is expressed as G (RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 -> RWs_torque_equalTo_0). No previous LTL properties are given, but any that fail to include the global (always) operator or don't enforce the direct implication at every point in time wouldn't capture the requirement as stringently.

The natural language statement expresses a requirement that whenever the signal 'RWs_angular_momentum' is greater than 0.35, 'RWs_torque' must be equal to 0. This is a standard implication type requirement and can be captured in LTL as a global property: G (RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 -> RWs_torque_equalTo_0). This means that at all times, if RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 is true, then RWs_torque_equalTo_0 must also hold in that moment. This matches the intent of the requirement. No previous properties are given for comparison.
